title: Negative Onboarding Debt

content.question: What might your business's onboarding be teaching users wrong about what your business does or how it works?
content.equation: NOD = Wrong Model × Correction Cost × Time-to-Correction
sop: List the 2-3 things a brand-new customer might misunderstand about your product or business in their first session.
Rate the severity of the Wrong Model 1-10 for each, how far off their mental model is from reality (see calculation, page 5).
Rate the Correction Cost 1-10, how much effort it takes to fix that misunderstanding once it's set (see calculation, page 5).
Rate the Time-to-Correction 1-10, how long that wrong idea typically persists before anyone catches it (see calculation, page 5).
Multiply the three scores for your worst misunderstanding (see calculation, page 5), and fix your onboarding copy or flow directly at that point first.
content.explainer: A confused first user is more expensive than an unacquired one. They churn and tell others.
questions: What might your business's onboarding be teaching users wrong about what your business does or how it works?
If a new customer's very first impression of how to use your product is slightly wrong, how long does that wrong idea usually stick around?
Would it cost you more to fix a confused customer's misunderstanding now, or to let it turn into a churned customer later?
content.equation_example: Wrong Model rated 7 out of 10, Correction Cost rated 6 out of 10, Time-to-Correction rated 8 out of 10. 7 times 6 times 8 equals 336. Higher means that early misunderstanding is expensive and slow to fix, a real threat to retention. Lower means even if a new user gets it wrong at first, it's cheap and quick to correct.
content.closing_statement: A confused first impression is expensive to unlearn later.

caption:
Principle Nº 85: Negative Onboarding Debt

Early confusion compounds long-term problems. A confused first user is more expensive than an unacquired one.

Ask yourself: What might your business's onboarding be teaching users wrong about what your business does or how it works?
